interactions.summary: Jiang says the backlash may include operatives digging for dirt, that old claims about his China arrest are being repurposed, and that zealous actors believe they must cross a world-historical finish line even if it kills the world.

The host brings in his research on New Haven and Yale: hyper-Calvinist millenarian founders, a planned nine-square city grid, Kabbalah/magic-square overlays, Yale's Hebrew symbolism, and Skull and Bones, then asks who is moving the Iran chess pieces toward an end-times goal.

Jiang says the host's Yale history makes intuitive sense because Yale feels missionary and religiously feverish, unlike Harvard's success drive or Princeton's aristocratic style; he connects Yale to CIA and national-security zealots, then introduces Sabbatean Frankism/Chabad-Lubavitch as another faction in the one-world-government plan.

speaker_notes.note: The host's long question contains his own research claims and should be separated from Jiang's answer in later public questions.

claims.claim: Jiang says coordinated backlash may involve digging up old accusations from his China arrest even though he frames that arrest as journalism work from about twenty-five years earlier.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's concern during the week of this interview.

claims.claim: Jiang says the actors behind the current crisis are religious zealots who believe they are near a finish line of bringing God to Earth, regardless of destruction.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's diagnosis of the eschatological crisis.

claims.claim: Jiang characterizes Yale as missionary and religiously feverish, with a tradition of producing zealots for the CIA and national-security apparatus.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's retrospective institutional diagnosis of Yale.

claims.claim: Jiang introduces Sabbatean Frankism, which he associates with Chabad-Lubavitch, as a faction aligned with Freemasonry in pursuit of one-world government.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's faction map in this interview.

signature_moments.moment: The conspirators are not merely strategic; they are marathon runners at an apocalyptic finish line.
signature_moments.source_phrase: We have to cross the finish line
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's crisis model its emotional charge: rational strategy becomes religious compulsion.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's characterization of Yale as animated by religious fever and institutional mission, distinct from Harvard's success culture and Princeton's aristocracy.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates the PBS worker-protest arrest approximately twenty-five years before this 2026 interview.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders 'Sabbatean Frankists' inconsistently as 'Sapien Frankis'; use the historically recognizable term in summaries with caution.
